text: The camera lens is cleaned, revealing the seminal vesicles and vas deferens. The surgeon deflates the Foley catheter balloon and prepares to make an incision higher up, following the nerve-sparing technique.

The surgeon operates using an XI robot with cautery and bipolar settings at two, while maintaining pneumoperitoneum at twelve using an air seal device. The assistant pushes down the peritoneum as the surgeon locates the posterior layer, visibly lifting the bladder upward.

The surgeon performs a posterior dissection, lifting the bladder or seminal vesicle from the rectum to reveal a clear plane. The vas deferens is visible, and the ureter travels along this plane. The surgeon identifies the trigone and considers going one plane deeper.

The surgeon clips the right vas deferens and begins dissecting the seminal vesicles. The posterior denonvilliers fascia is visible, and a suction device is used to push down the tissue. The prostate weighs 47 grams, and the surgeon aims to dissect as far down as possible to facilitate the procedure.

The surgeon visually identifies the seminal vesicle and begins to decompress it by puncturing, aiding in the dissection of its tip. The seminal vesicle appears dilated and engorged, which is common in patients who have not ejaculated for a long time, making dissection challenging.

The surgeon is positioned on top of the neurovascular bundle on the right side, attempting to carefully dissect down the neurovascular bundle while addressing adhesions at the level of the Denonvilliers' fascia. The prostate capsule is visible, with cancer located on the left side, prompting a wider dissection on both the left and right.

The surgeon performs an interfacial technique on the right side, preparing for a limited dissection on the left side due to organ-confined disease. The left side vas deferens is taken down, followed by the dissection and decompression of the seminal vesicle. Small arterioles at the tip of the seminal vesicles are clipped.

The surgeon clips two arteries after decompressing the left seminal vesicle, minimizing the need for counter traction. The tip of the seminal vesicle is nearly exposed, and the surgeon applies a clip to secure the area.
The surgeon uses forceps to manipulate tissue in the denouement fascia area, ensuring safety near the rectal wall. The right-handed surgeon prefers to start dissection on the right side.

The surgeon opens veins around the prostate, with the neurovascular bundle already addressed. The surgeon moves laterally, lifting the bladder away from the prostate to reveal the junction where the bladder attaches to the prostate. The surgeon demonstrates a clear plane between these structures, preparing to clip the last portions of the neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon carefully avoids damaging the levator muscles while performing an interfacial dissection on the right side. The neurovascular bundles are visible on the lateral side. The surgeon mobilizes the area to find the plane between the prostate and bladder, noting a small nodule on the bladder near the prostate.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to manipulate the bladder, making a higher incision to prevent it from obstructing the surgical field. The dissection focuses on the left side of the prostate, carefully isolating and cutting through part of the neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon performs an extra fascial dissection on the left side, carefully navigating outside the venous complexes while preserving the levator muscles and maximizing nerve preservation. The neurovascular bundle, approximately 1.5 centimeters in length, is nearly completed. Robotic forceps are used to re-grab the area, lifting the prostate to expose its lateral portions. Metal clips are applied to secure the anterior side without dissecting the posterior side.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection on the left side, pushing the bladder away from the prostate to identify the urethra. The neurovascular bundle is visible, and counter traction is applied to better appreciate the urethra in the apical area.

The surgeon is working in the dorsal vein area near the urethra, preparing to remove the Foley catheter. The dissection around the urethra is minimized to reduce surgical trauma and preserve continence. The surgeon appreciates the dorsal vein and begins cutting the urethra using a lateral approach.

The surgeon is left with the bladder and the left side of the prostate, which appears visually clear and well-defined. A small nodule is identified, and the surgeon prepares to excise it. The anatomical planes are followed, facilitating a smooth dissection process.

The surgeon uses a balloon technique to gently compress the dorsal veins, applying wet clips to secure the area. The French bag is visible as the team prepares for anastomosis. The needle driver is switched to the left hand, and the pro grasp is used to lift the bladder, with the procedure continuing under a zero-degree lens.

The surgeon inserts a needle and pushes the Foley catheter in, then deflates it. Perineal pressure is applied. The surgeon begins suturing the dorsal vein using a figure-of-eight stitch to control bleeding. The procedure is viewed through a zero-degree lens, focusing on the twelve o'clock position of the bladder. Sutures are placed at the twelve and eleven o'clock positions, using a V-lock suture with a larger needle to facilitate anastomosis.

The surgeon applies constant perineal pressure while removing the Foley catheter. Sutures are placed at the two o'clock and three o'clock positions on the bladder neck. The surgeon discusses the impact of bladder neck size on continence, noting that urethral closure pressure is more critical. A four-thirty suture is placed, followed by the use of a new needle for an eleven o'clock suture.

The surgeon sutures the bladder neck at the eleven o'clock position, followed by the ten o'clock and nine o'clock positions, using V-lock sutures to control tension and prevent slippage. The Foley catheter is adjusted, and perineal pressure is maintained steadily.

The surgeon performs an inside-out stitch on the urethra, locking the suture to facilitate anastomosis. The bladder is nearly cinched down, and additional stitches are thrown while ensuring the Foley catheter is not caught in the process. The catheter is moved slightly to confirm it remains free from the sutures.

The surgeon makes an incision at the junction of the vas deferens and epigastric vessels, preparing for a limited lymph node dissection on the left side. The peritoneum is opened minimally to preserve the surrounding structures.

The surgeon performs minimal dissection without using clips, applying gentle force to push down the tissue. Surgical cells are prepared for use. The technique is demonstrated safely, resembling an extended node dissection similar to bladder cancer procedures.
The surgeon performs a sampling biopsy, using forceps to manipulate tissue. Surgical cells are packed into the area for safety, with adjustments made to the size of the packing material.
